metrics.name: Accounting correctness
metrics.desc:
Accounting model type,Pooled reserve accounting
Deposits represented as,aTokens with balances increasing as yield accrues
Borrows represented as,Non-transferable debt tokens representing accrued debt
Interest accrual mechanism,Index-based accounting
Reserve accounting model,Shared Aave v3 reserve logic
Fee accrual mechanism,Borrower interest is split between suppliers and protocol reserves
Bad debt accounting,Bad debt is handled through reserve deficit / coverage mechanisms
Shared accounting logic across markets,Yes
Market-specific accounting customizations,Yes
Use math-sensitive logic,Yes
Upgradeable,Yes
Known historical accounting bugs,No
Operation evidence at scale,Strong
Public documentation,Yes

metrics.name: Accounting correctness
metrics.desc:
Accounting architecture is mature, public, and heavily reviewed.
aToken/debt-token accounting is well understood and battle-tested.
No current evidence points to a live Aave v3 core accounting exploit.
Score is not 1 because the system is upgradeable, index-based, multi-market, and highly configurable.

metrics.name: Liquidation reliability
metrics.desc:
Liquidation trigger mechanism,Health Factor below 1
Solvency threshold definition,HF ≥ 1 is solvent, HF < 1 is liquidatable
Liquidations permissionless,Yes
Liquidation type,Partial or full
Close factor design,Dynamic close factor
Liquidation incentive structure,Debt repayment in exchange for collateral plus liquidation bonus
Maximum liquidation size per transaction,Up to 50% normally, up to 100% under deeper stress or small positions
Liquidation logic uniform or asset-specific,Shared core logic with asset-specific parameters
Dependence on third-party liquidators,Yes
Dependence on external liquidity venues for unwind,Yes
Robustness under high volatility,Moderate
Robustness under correlated collateral stress,Moderate
Robustness under high gas or congestion,Moderate
Presence of caps, isolation modes, or circuit breakers,Yes
Historical liquidation performance during stress events,Mixed
Historical bad debt attributable to liquidation failure,No clear core liquidation-code failure
Documentation describing liquidation mechanism and edge cases,Yes

metrics.name: Liquidation reliability
metrics.desc:
The liquidation design is mature, permissionless, and economically incentivized.
Liquidation logic itself was not shown to have failed in the rsETH/Kelp event.
The rsETH/Kelp event should not be counted as direct evidence of liquidation-mechanism failure.
Residual risk remains because liquidations depend on oracle correctness, liquidator capacity, gas, repay-asset liquidity, and collateral exit liquidity.

metrics.name: Oracle integrity
metrics.desc:
Oracle providers used,Chainlink, CAPO, and asset-specific oracle sources
Oracle type by asset,Direct feeds for standard assets, custom or capped logic for correlated assets
Pricing model type,Direct, derived, capped, or custom
Heartbeat or update frequency,Feed-specific
Deviation thresholds,Feed-specific
Fallback oracle mechanisms,Yes
Stale-price protections,Partial
Oracle pause or sentinel logic,Yes
Assets requiring custom pricing logic,Yes
Dependence on a small number of oracle providers,Yes
Complexity of oracle stack,Moderate to high
Governance ability to change oracle paths,Yes
Historical oracle incidents or misconfigurations,Yes
Evidence of oracle performance during prior stress events,Mixed
Public documentation describing oracle architecture,Yes
Oracle assumptions simple or model-dependent,Model-dependent for correlated assets and LRTs

metrics.name: Oracle integrity
metrics.desc:
Oracle infrastructure is strong relative to most DeFi protocols.
Chainlink, CAPO, fallback oracle, and sentinel logic are meaningful mitigants.
Main weakness is model/configuration risk for correlated assets, LSTs, LRTs, and bridged assets.
The rsETH/Kelp event is most appropriately reflected here because it exposed collateral accounting and oracle/model assumptions rather than core accounting failure.

metrics.name: Governance and upgrade safety
metrics.desc:
Core contracts upgradeable,Yes
Entities able to upgrade contracts,Aave governance / governed admin paths
Entities able to change risk parameters,Risk Admin and Pool Admin
Entities able to list or remove collateral,Asset Listing Admin and Pool Admin
Entities able to change oracle configuration,Asset Listing Admin and Pool Admin
Entities able to pause or freeze protocol activity,Emergency Admin and Pool Admin
Separation of powers across roles,Yes
Roles multisig-controlled,Yes
Timelocks in place,Yes
Emergency paths bypass normal governance delays,Yes
Governance changes transparent and documented,Yes
Review, veto, or guardian mechanisms,Yes
Historical major governance actions,Yes
Historical governance or configuration incidents,Yes
Scope of privileged roles over solvency-critical functions,Broad
Ease with which governance can introduce system-breaking changes,Moderate

metrics.name: Governance and upgrade safety
metrics.desc:
Governance is mature, public, and structured.
Timelocks, guardians, service providers, and public execution reduce governance and upgrade risk.
Privileged roles are broad, but they are also part of the protocol’s risk-management process.
The rsETH response showed governance/emergency mechanisms working, not failing, so this anchor should not be scored as medium-risk solely because privileged controls exist.

metrics.name: Upgradeability and admin control
metrics.desc:
Core contracts upgradeable,Yes
Upgrade mechanism,Proxy/address-provider model
Entities able to upgrade core contracts,Aave governance / governed admin paths
Timelocks apply to upgrades,Yes
Emergency upgrade paths exist,No unrestricted emergency upgrade path identified
Emergency pause/freeze paths exist,Yes
Upgrade authority multisig-controlled,Partially
Upgrade authority concentrated or distributed,Distributed at governance layer, more concentrated at guardian/admin layer
Upgrade scope narrow or broad,Broad
Upgrades can affect solvency-critical functions,Yes
Historical use of upgrade powers,Yes
Upgrade process publicly documented,Yes
Review requirements before execution,Yes
Governance can block or veto upgrades,Yes
Ability to execute urgent changes outside standard process,Yes, for emergency/risk actions

metrics.name: Upgradeability and admin control
metrics.desc:
Upgradeability is broad enough to affect core protocol behavior.
Governance and timelocks reduce risk.
Emergency powers are useful, but add discretionary trust.
Net effect is a moderate negative modifier.

metrics.name: Governance execution integrity
metrics.desc:
Governance model,Aave Governance v3
Proposal process publicly documented,Yes
Review process for major changes,Yes
Independent risk or security review involved,Yes
Onchain and offchain governance responsibilities separated,Yes
Governance actions transparent and publicly visible,Yes
Review or veto mechanisms exist,Yes
Minimum delay before execution,Yes
Emergency governance path exists,Yes
Governance has made solvency-critical changes historically,Yes
Historical evidence of prudent governance under stress,Yes
Historical governance execution errors or reversals,No major recent governance execution exploit found
Governance process stable or ad hoc,Stable
Operational responsibility for approved changes clearly defined,Yes

metrics.name: Governance execution integrity
metrics.desc:
Governance execution is stronger than average.
Public process, timelocks, and veto mechanisms reduce risk.
Cross-chain execution and emergency overlays add complexity.
Net effect is a mild positive modifier.

metrics.name: Governance power concentration
metrics.desc:
Governance bodies involved,Aave DAO, delegates, tokenholders, executors, guardians, service providers, and admin roles
Tokenholders govern critical protocol surfaces,Yes
Effective decision power concentrated or distributed,Mixed
Delegation concentration,Meaningful
Voting participation level,Strong
Small group can determine outcomes,Partially
Small group controls execution layer,Partially
Governance participation open or restricted,Open at voting layer, restricted at emergency/admin layer
Critical roles shared across independent actors,Yes
Timelocks or review delays reduce concentration risk,Yes
Public visibility into governance concentration,Partial
Historical evidence of coordinated malicious control,No
Historical evidence of coordinated emergency action,Yes

metrics.name: Governance power concentration
metrics.desc:
Aave is meaningfully decentralized at the governance layer.
Effective control is still partly concentrated through delegates, guardians, and admin roles.
The concentration risk is moderate, not severe.
Mild negative modifier is appropriate.

metrics.name: Operational complexity
metrics.desc:
Major onchain components required for normal operation,Many
Major offchain components required for normal operation,Many
Oracle dependencies,Yes
Dependence on external liquidators or keepers,Yes
Cross-market dependencies,Yes
Cross-chain dependencies,Yes
Cross-module dependencies,Yes
Human operational intervention required,Yes
Monitoring and reconciliation requirements,High
Number of distinct actors needed for safe operation,High
Operational workflows simple or multi-step,Multi-step
Manual execution required for critical actions,Yes
Failure in one component materially disrupts protocol operation,Yes
Historical incidents linked to operational coordination failure,Yes
Operational architecture publicly documented,Yes
Internal escalation playbooks fully public,No

metrics.name: Operational complexity
metrics.desc:
This is one of the strongest upward modifiers.
Aave v3 is mature, but operationally complex.
Multi-chain markets, custom oracle paths, external liquidators, and collateral issuers expand the failure surface.
The rsETH incident makes this risk concrete rather than theoretical.

metrics.name: Privileged roles scope
metrics.desc:
Admin roles identified,Yes
Emergency roles identified,Yes
Oracle roles identified,Yes
Risk parameter roles identified,Yes
Asset listing roles identified,Yes
Roles affecting pause or freeze behavior,Yes
Roles affecting liquidation-critical parameters,Yes
Roles affecting solvency-critical configuration,Yes
Roles affecting token implementations,Yes
Roles multisig-controlled,Yes
Timelocks apply to role actions,Partially
Role permissions narrow or broad,Broad
Roles can override standard protocol behavior,Yes
Separation of duties across privileged roles,Yes
Historical use of privileged powers,Yes
Privileged actions transparent and publicly traceable,Generally yes

metrics.name: Privileged roles scope
metrics.desc:
Role separation is a positive.
But privileged roles can materially affect solvency-critical behavior.
Pool Admin and emergency/risk roles are especially important.
This creates a meaningful trust/configuration risk.

metrics.name: Audits
metrics.desc:
Public audits available,Yes
Audit coverage breadth,Broad
Audit firms,Multiple independent firms
Core protocol contracts audited,Yes
Accounting-critical logic audited,Yes
Liquidation logic audited,Yes
Oracle-related integrations audited,Partially
Governance or upgrade paths audited,Partially
Findings remediated,Generally yes
Reports public,Yes
Audits recent or outdated,Recent and historical
Important unaudited protocol surfaces,Market liquidity, external collateral backing, live oracle assumptions, and emergency operations
Re-audits performed after major upgrades,Yes
Audit scope covers current architecture,Broadly yes
Audit scope covers all live market configurations,No

metrics.name: Audits
metrics.desc:
Audit depth is a major strength.
Recent v3.x reviews improve confidence.
Audit coverage mostly reduces smart-contract implementation risk.
It does not fully cover oracle-model, collateral, governance, and operational risks.

metrics.name: Formal verification
metrics.desc:
Formal verification disclosed,Yes
Verified contract scope,Core Aave v3 and later v3.x components
Verified properties,Core accounting, reserve, token, and implementation properties
Core accounting invariants formally verified,Yes
Liquidation-critical logic formally verified,Partially
Governance or upgrade invariants formally verified,Partially
Public verification reports,Yes
Verification covers current deployed architecture,Meaningfully, but not fully
Verification limited to narrow components or broad core system,Broad core system
Full end-to-end economic verification,No

metrics.name: Formal verification
metrics.desc:
Formal verification is a real assurance strength.
It meaningfully reduces core-code and accounting uncertainty.
It does not fully cover liquidator behavior, collateral backing, oracle economics, or governance operations.
Strong but not maximum credit.

metrics.name: Testing
metrics.desc:
Public test repositories,Yes
Unit tests,Yes
Integration tests,Yes
Invariant tests,Yes
Fuzzing,Yes / partially evidenced
Fork simulations,Yes / used in practice

Coverage of accounting logic,Strong
Coverage of liquidation logic,Strong at contract level
Coverage of oracle integrations,Partial
Coverage of governance or upgrade flows,Partial
Coverage publicly consolidated,No
Testing updated after major protocol changes,Yes
Evidence of realistic production-like testing,Yes
Full coverage of live collateral failure modes,No

metrics.name: Testing
metrics.desc:
Testing appears strong and multi-layered.
Release-specific reviews and invariant testing add confidence.
Testing cannot fully reproduce live market liquidity, collateral failure, or governance response.
Strong positive modifier, but not maximum.

metrics.name: Monitoring
metrics.desc:
Public dashboards available,Yes
Monitoring of protocol solvency metrics,Yes
Monitoring of liquidation activity,Partial
Monitoring of oracle behavior,Partial
Monitoring of bad debt or reserve stress,Partial
Monitoring of governance actions,Yes
Monitoring of privileged role actions,Onchain traceable
Incident detection tooling disclosed,Yes
Observability documentation,Partial
Monitoring public, internal, or both,Both
Evidence of monitoring supporting incident response,Yes
Coverage across critical protocol surfaces,Good
Internal alerting thresholds fully public,No
Internal escalation playbooks fully public,No

metrics.name: Monitoring
metrics.desc:
Monitoring is a clear positive.
Public dashboards and governance visibility reduce blind spots.
rsETH response shows active monitoring and operational response.
Internal alerting thresholds are not fully public, so credit is moderate rather than maximum.

metrics.name: Incident history
metrics.desc:
Major protocol-wide incidents identified,No recent Aave v3 core smart-contract compromise found
Major market-specific incidents identified,Yes
Main recent incident,April 2026 rsETH/Kelp event
Historic vulnerabilities identified and resolved,Yes
Consolidated official incident log,No
Public disclosure quality,High
Post-mortems published,Yes
Repeated incident pattern exists,External collateral, oracle/model, and configuration risk
Time since last major incident,Very short
Incidents resulted in insolvency or contained disruption,Material modeled bad-debt scenarios
Core Aave contracts compromised in recent incident,No
Evidence of effective remediation after incidents,Yes
Evidence that lessons changed controls or design,Likely, still evolving

metrics.name: Incident history
metrics.desc:
Aave’s core-contract incident history is strong relative to DeFi.
The recent rsETH event prevents a favorable incident-history credit.
Modeled bad debt was material even though the issue was external to Aave contracts.
Until final remediation is clear, a mild negative modifier is appropriate.

metrics.name: Operational changes history
metrics.desc:
Material protocol upgrades historically,Yes
Material changes to liquidation design,Limited
Material changes to oracle design,Yes
Material changes to governance structure,Yes
Material changes to privileged role design,Yes
Material changes to market configuration,Yes
Changes orderly and documented,Generally yes
Changes audited or independently reviewed,Yes
Changes introduced incidents,Not clearly from code upgrades
Configuration changes introduced exposure,Yes
Rollback or mitigation mechanisms exist,Yes
Upgrade playbooks or execution procedures documented,Partially
Overall change history stable or high-churn,Moderate to high churn
Evidence of disciplined rollout of critical changes,Yes
Live configuration risk remains,Yes

metrics.name: Operational changes history
metrics.desc:
Aave’s change process is disciplined and public.
The protocol still changes frequently across assets, chains, parameters, and oracle paths.
Frequent change creates configuration risk.
Small upward modifier is justified.

metrics.name: System maturity and production track record
metrics.desc:
Initial deployment date of protocol,Multi-year production history
Initial deployment date of current architecture,Aave v3 live since 2022 / Ethereum Core since 2023
Time in production under meaningful usage,Long
Number of market cycles survived,Multiple
Current architecture recent or long-lived,Long-lived core model, actively upgraded
Accounting model maturity,High
Liquidation model maturity,High
Governance model maturity,High
Production usage substantial,Yes
Long-term operational stability evidence,Strong
Evidence of safe operation during severe market stress,Mixed
Current architecture materially different from early versions,Yes
Maturity eliminates collateral/oracle/governance risk,No

metrics.name: System maturity and production track record
metrics.desc:
Aave v3 has meaningful production maturity.
Core accounting and liquidation models are widely used and well understood.
Maturity lowers unknown-unknown risk.
It does not eliminate collateral, oracle, governance, and cross-chain operational risk.
